does: This paper proposes a practical methodology for identifying and prioritizing loss-of-control mitigations in national security AI deployments by working backward from benchmark errors. The approach involves three steps: evaluating AI systems on mission-specific benchmarks that approximate real use cases, analyzing incorrect responses the system produces, and backchaining to determine which affordances and permissions would have prevented those errors. The method grounds mitigation prioritization in concrete evidence of failure modes rather than abstract threat modeling, enabling deployers to immediately begin building tailored safeguards based on their own generated benchmark data rather than waiting for comprehensive pre-deployment evaluations or safety cases.
connects: This work extends prior research on AI safety mechanisms including affordances-based approaches to capability restriction and permission-based access controls, building on structured threat modeling traditions in security engineering. It responds to the growing literature on loss-of-control risks in advanced AI systems and complements existing mitigation strategies like pre-deployment agentic evaluations, post-deployment monitoring, and formal AI safety cases. The methodology draws on benchmark-driven evaluation practices increasingly common in ML safety research while connecting to national security-specific concerns about AI system failures in high-stakes contexts, where mission-specific evaluation frameworks already exist but lack systematic linkage to mitigation design.
enables: This empirical backchaining approach enables national security organizations to immediately operationalize loss-of-control mitigations without waiting for foundational theoretical breakthroughs or comprehensive safety infrastructure. It supports iterative refinement of affordances and permissions based on real failure evidence, potentially scaling mitigation development across multiple mission domains and deployment contexts. The methodology could facilitate continuous improvement cycles where post-deployment errors inform tighter constraints on future versions, and it may catalyze development of more sophisticated mission-specific benchmarks that better capture realistic failure modes. Future work could extend this to quantifying mitigation effectiveness, comparing affordance-permission tradeoffs, and integrating backchaining results into formal safety cases for high-assurance deployments.
